It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Transparency in transnational governance: The determinants of information disclosure of voluntary sustainability programs Philip Schleifer Department of Political Science, University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands Matteo Fiorini Robert Schuman Centre for Advanced Studies, European University Institute, Villa Schifanoia, Florence, Italy Graeme Auld School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, Ottawa, Canada Abstract The rise of “new” transnational governance has intensiﬁed debates about a lack of accountability in global politics. Reviewing the mechanisms through which transparency can foster accountability beyond the state, this article explores the determinants of information disclosure in the ﬁeld of transnational sustainability governance. Examining the institutional design of 113 vol- untary sustainability programs, we ﬁnd a positive correlation between the involvement of public actors and information dis- closure. In contrast, the role of civil society is more ambiguous. There is no statistical support for arguments linking non- governmental organization participation to increased transparency. At the same time, our analysis reveals a robust correlation between civil society-led metagovernance and information disclosure. Moreover, we ﬁnd that crowding has a negative effect on transparency, whereas normative peer pressures have no inﬂuence. At a broader level, the analysis reveals a lack of “deep transparency” among transnational sustainability governors. This limits the scope for transparency-induced accountability in this policy domain. Keywords: accountability, institutional design, sustainability, transnational governance, transparency. 1. Introduction When it comes to the governance of transnational production, “the state is far from the only game in town” (Abbott & Snidal 2009a, p. 87). This is particularly true of the ﬁeld of global sustainability politics where private and hybrid governance arrangements have proliferated in recent years. Prominent examples are the Forest Stew- ardship Council (FSC) and the Rainforest Alliance. Created by non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and ﬁrms (sometimes with the involvement of governments), these voluntary sustainability programs (VSPs) set stan- dards for transnational production, operate veriﬁcation systems, and feature quasi-judicial conﬂict resolution mechanisms. In this and other policy areas, they have taken over a wide range of governance functions that used to be the prerogative of states and international organizations (IOs). This shift from state-centered to polycentric governance has intensiﬁed debates about a lack of accountability in global politics (Grant & Keohane 2005; Bäckstrand 2006; Buchanan & Keohane 2006; Black 2008; Biermann & Gupta 2011; Kramarz & Park 2016; Bäckstrand et al. 2018). Of particular concern is the rapidly expanding realm of transnational private governance, with its many actors and diffuse authority structures. Here, traditional notions of accountability no longer apply (Dingwerth 2007). In search of alternatives, transparency is often hailed as a possible solution. Transparency, it is argued, can foster accountability in transnational governance by Correspondence: Philip Schleifer, Department of Political Science, University of Amsterdam, Nieuwe Achtergracht 166, 1018 WV Amsterdam, Netherlands. Others, however, are more skeptical. They critically scrutinize the ability of transparency to empower accounting actors in global governance. Transparency may indeed not be a silver bullet, but even these critical scholars continue to view it as a positive force (Gupta 2010; Mol 2010; Gupta & Mason 2014). While the current discussion is mainly centered on the effects of transparency, less is known about its sources. If transparency matters (and the broader debate seems to suggest just that), we need to better understand its ori- gins. Several studies have examined the sources of transparency in the context of IOs (Mitchell 1998; Grigorescu 2007); however, in the realm of transnational governance, these relationships remain underresearched.1 Given these gaps in our understanding of the issue, this article explores the determinants of information disclosure in global sustainability politics – a policy domain that has seen a “Cambrian explosion” of new transnational gover- nance in recent years (Abbott 2012). To shed light on the issue, we analyze a dataset of 113 VSPs and develop a new measure of transparency. With a focus on VSP’s operations, we describe their transparency practices in four areas: decisionmaking, stan- dard setting, veriﬁcation, and dispute settlement. Our results reveal that many VSPs are “shallow” in their trans- parency – that is, they disclose little about the ways in which they work in practice. We argue that this limits the scope for transparency-induced accountability in this governance domain. At the same time, we observe signiﬁ- cant interprogram variation. To investigate these differences, we review the broader literature on transparency, combining insights from studies on domestic politics, IOs, and transnational governance in order to develop six hypotheses about the internal and external determinants of information disclosure, which we test in a multivari- ate analysis. The analysis reveals a positive correlation between the involvement of public actors in VSPs’ central governance bodies and information disclosure. The role of civil society is more ambiguous. There is no statistical support for the widely held belief that direct NGO participation increases transparency levels. However, the anal- ysis reveals a robust correlation between civil society-led metagovernance and information disclosure. Moreover, we ﬁnd that crowding has a negative inﬂuence on transparency, whereas normative peer pressure has no signiﬁ- cant effect. We interpret these ﬁndings in light of existing studies on transnational governance, complementing the mostly qualitative literature on the subject.2 The remainder of this article is organized in seven sections. Section 2 revisits the debate on accountability and transparency in transnational governance. Section 3 introduces the concept of transparency and presents the results of the descriptive analysis. In sections 4 and 5, we introduce and operationalize our hypotheses and dis- cuss our empirical model. Section 6 and 7 present the results of the multivariate analysis and interpret our main ﬁndings. A ﬁnal section concludes. 2. Accountability and transparency in transnational governance At the beginning of the 1990s, the end of the Cold War ushered in an era of transnational governance, opening up space for non-state actors to play a more salient role in international affairs (Rosenau 1992). While the growth of formal IOs has slowed, the population of private and hybrid governance arrangements has increased exponen- tially (Abbott & Snidal 2009a; Abbott et al. 2016). This is particularly true in the ﬁeld of sustainability governance where growth of the number of private and hybrid governance arrangements has accelerated over the last 20 years (Abbott 2012). One important ﬁrst-mover program was the FSC (Cashore et al. 2004). Created in 1993 by a coali- tion of environmental NGOs and ﬁrms, the program sets standards for sustainable forestry management and operates a certiﬁcation program with global reach. Since then, a great variety of VSPs have been created in a wide range of industry sectors. Other examples include well-known programs like the Roundtable on Sustainable Palm Oil and Rainforest Alliance. However, there are also many less visible VSPs, such as Fair Flowers Fair Plants or the ProTerra Foundation. Figure 1 illustrates this trend over time based on our research sample (see discussion). Among scholars of international relations (IR), the proliferation of new modes of governance has intensiﬁed debates about the prospects and limits of accountability beyond the state (Grant & Keohane 2005; Bäckstrand 2006; Dingwerth 2007; Black 2008; Hale 2008; Biermann & Gupta 2011; Kramarz & Park 2016; Bäckstrand et al. 2018). One important concern is that global governance institutions are too distant and detached from citizens. The result is a growing accountability deﬁcit as more and more decisionmaking authority is transferred from the national to international and transnational levels (Dingwerth 2007). © 2019 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 P. Schleifer, et al. Transparency in transnational governance Page 3: The problem, in a nutshell, is that at the national level, an important source of democratic legitimacy is what Grant and Keohane (2005, p. 29) call principal–agent accountability. In this model, the principal (people) has the right and ability to hold its agent (government) to account, to judge whether it has fulﬁlled its responsibilities, and to impose sanctions if these responsibilities have not been met. In democratically constituted states, the main mechanism to achieve this is through periodic general elections. However, in governance beyond the state, principal–agent accountability of this type runs into difﬁculties. In the realm of transnational governance, the model does not work. Here, no principal (or global demos) exists, governors are typically self-selected, and no electoral mechanism is available to hold them to account (Dryzek 2000; Dingwerth 2007). In this context, much hope has been put on the concept of transparency (Florini 2003; Hale 2008). Hale noted“[i]f ‘democracy deﬁcit’ is the catchphrase for global governance’s problem, ‘transparency’ is its buzzword solution” (2008, p. 73). But what role can transparency play in mitigating the problem? An answer to this ques- tion requires unpacking the concepts of accountability and transparency and conducting a discussion on how they are related. According to Schedler, the concept of accountability has two components: answerability – “the right to receive information and the corresponding obligation to release details” – and enforcement – “the idea that accounting actors do not just ‘call into question’ but also eventually punish improper behavior” (1999, p. 13). As summarized by Hale (2008), A is thus accountable to B if B can (i) know A’s behavior; and (ii) exert pressure on A to inﬂu- ence that behavior. Regarding the ﬁrst component of accountability, the importance of transparency for establish- ing accountability is clear. Without reliable information, answerability is impossible. However, the role of transparency in enforcement is less obvious and also more controversial. Optimists argue, “transparency is providing new opportunities both to enforce rules and standards and to hold accountable those who purport to act in the public interest” (Florini 2003, p. 196). But how does this work in practice? In Full Disclosure: The Perils and Promise of Transparency, Fung et al. (2008) describe a “transpar- ency action cycle” in which information disclosure triggers constructive behavioral change. Their causal model proceeds in four stages: (i) a discloser (e.g. a company) discloses information (e.g. pollution data) that is relevant and salient to users (e.g. consumers); (ii) users act in response to this information; (iii) the discloser is sensitive to users’ actions; and (iv) responds constructively. From an enforcement perspective, the interesting question is: What mechanisms of behavioral change (stages 2–4) does transparency trigger? And how effective are they? Reviewing the literature on transnational governance, Hale (2008, pp. 76–87) identiﬁes three main mecha- nisms that accounting actors can use to hold targeted institutions accountable. First, market pressure plays an important role. For example, consumers, investors, and NGOs can respond to information disclosure by changing 0 20 40 60 80 100 113 Cumulative number of programs 1967 1970 1973 1976 1979 1982 1985 1988 1991 1994 1997 2000 2003 2006 2009 2012 2014 Year Figure 1 Proliferation of voluntary sustainability programs over time. © 2019 The Authors. Regulation & Governance Published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Transparency in transnational governance P. Schleifer, et al. Page 4: their consumption and investment decisions or by launching corporate shame campaigns. These actions can unfold a coercive force if they threaten to have material consequences for the target actor. Second, there are methods of “soft enforcement” through public discourse. Based on Habermasian discourse theory, the argument runs that transparency creates pressure to tell the truth, as it makes it easier to expose lies through the “forceless force of the better argument.” This can discourage rent seeking and other self-serving behavior. Finally, transpar- ency can facilitate behavioral change through enabling self-reﬂection. In this regard, information disclosure can reveal discrepancies between an actor’s internalized norms and its actual behavior and a desire to correct the mismatch. However, these mechanisms have their limitations, and scholars have expressed doubts about transparency and its ability to empower and enforce in transnational governance. For example, while acknowledging the importance of information for all forms of accountability, Grant and Keohane state “[w]ithout standards and sanctions (…) accountability that is both effective and widely viewed as legitimate will remain elusive” (2005, pp. 39–40). In addition, several scholars that have explored the role of transparency in global environmental gov- ernance have expressed skepticism about its ability to truly empower accounting actors (Gupta 2010; Mol 2010; Gupta & Mason 2014). Thus, as observed by Fox (2007), the relationship between transparency and accountabil- ity remains uncertain. Hard accountability that includes sanctions might indeed remain elusive in transnational governance. But like Hale, Fox believes that transparency can lead to softer forms of accountability. The upshot of this discussion is that transparency is not a panacea to the accountability deﬁcit of transna- tional governance (Grant & Keohane 2005). However, the disclosure of salient information may enable soft enforcement through market pressure, public discourse, and self-reﬂection. What seems signiﬁcant in this context is the “depth” of information that is being disclosed. By this we mean the degree to which the disclosed informa- tion allows insights into the actual workings of an institution, not just its formal procedures. As noted by Fox (2007), transparency that reveals little about the ways in which an institution works in practice will not do the job. But to be clear, we are not assuming that transparency is a sufﬁcient condition for generating accountability beyond the state. However, we follow Hale and others who argue that transparency is necessary for holding trans- national governors to account. 3. Conceptualizing and measuring transparency Given the centrality of transparency to the debate on accountability in transnational governance, our goal is to investigate it empirically. A look at the existing scholarship reveals that there are many different ways to study the phenomenon. For example, in the realm of domestic politics, there is a large body of literature focusing on the adoption and design of so-called freedom of information policies (e.g. Berliner 2014). In a similar way, scholars of IR have begun to analyze and compare the transparency policies of global governance institutions (Grigorescu 2003; Donaldson & Kingsbury 2013). Studying formal transparency polices has clear advantages. They are relatively easy to analyze and compare. However, there are also drawbacks. Most importantly, formal policies and actual practices often differ signiﬁcantly (Florini 2002). In the context of domestic governance, accounting actors can resort to legal mechanisms to enforce transparency policies. However, in the transnational realm this is not possible. Therefore, instead of formal policies, we focus on information disclosure practices. This perspective highlights the relational character of transparency. Studying it empirically requires speciﬁcation of what is being disclosed, by whom, and for whom (Grigorescu 2007; Gupta & Mason 2014, p. 5). Regarding the “what” and by “whom” questions, we focus our analysis on the transparency of VSPs about their operations. We construct our measure of transparency based on what we identify as their main activities in the regulatory process, namely: decisionmak- ing, standard setting, veriﬁcation, and dispute settlement. For each of these areas, two variables, representing dif- ferent degrees of transparency, are selected from a database (see discussion). We include measures for “shallow transparency” – disclosure of information about formal procedures – and “deep transparency” – disclosure of information about actual processes (see Table 1). In addition to identifying the discloser and the object of transparency, it is also important to deﬁne the target audience – that is, the group of actors to whom information is being disclosed (Grigorescu 2007, pp. 626–629). We focus on disclosure to the general public, which is the most unrestricted form of information disclosure. More © 2019 The Authors. Transparency in transnational governance Page 5: precisely, we focus on the open disclosure of information about decisionmaking, standard setting, veriﬁcation, and dispute settlement on VSPs websites. Overall, this leaves us with a clearly delineated concept of transparency for our investigation. To measure transparency empirically, we source data from the Standards Map of the International Trade Centre (ITC).3 Launched in 2011, the Standards Map database (SMD) is an inventory of VSPs in the ﬁeld of trade and production. In October 2018, the SMD included 247 VSPs operating in more than 120 product groups and 180 countries. It is one of the most comprehensive resources on VSPs and contains information about their standards, geographic scope, and organizational processes (including detailed information about their disclosure practices, as listed in Table 1).4 According to the ITC’s data collection protocols, the VSPs included in the database all satisfy the following minimum criteria: existence of a published set of criteria and indicators; existence of an implementation system; and coverage of at least one sustainability area (environment, social, economic and management, quality manage- ment, or ethics and integrity). In addition, the SMD includes transnational and domestic programs as well as VSPs with varying sponsorship arrangements (e.g. industry, civil society, and multistakeholder).5 One important limitation of the SMD is its weak coverage of certain types of VSPs, particularly ﬁrm-level programs. Several company codes of conduct are listed; however, the SMD’s coverage in this area cannot be con- sidered representative. A second limitation of the SMD is its “snapshot character.” As updates are performed at annual intervals, the information it contains reﬂects the state of affairs at the latest update. This limits the possi- bility to use the SMD to investigate trends and dynamics over time. Thus, we are unable to examine arguments by, for example, Gupta and Mason (2014, p. 15) who hypothesize that processes of democratization and marketi- zation have driven the uptake of transparency in global sustainability governance.6 To gain access to the full SMD and to prepare and work with the data, we undertook three ﬁeld trips to ITC headquarters in Geneva in November 2015, February 2017, and October 2017. The dataset analyzed in this study is a subsample of 113 VSPs taken from the SMD. The following selection criteria were applied in the creation of the sample: (i) the program is operational; (ii) it has a discernible governance structure; (iii) it is transnational (i.e. it operates in more than one country); (iv) it is not a ﬁrm-level code of conduct; (v) it develops environmen- tal and/or social standards for global supply chains (no purely technical, food safety, or quality standards); and (vi) in the selection of our sample, we took into account that some VSPs are listed with multiple standards in the SMD. The vast difference between shallow and deep transparency is particularly striking. The latter is much less frequent, and this is true for all four areas of operation. A common sense explanation would be that disclosing sensitive information about actual processes (as opposed to formal procedures) is associated with higher costs for the discloser – for example, by enabling more thorough public scrutiny. Here, the vast majority of VSPs (> 75%) do not disclose information at a deep level of transparency. However, as shown above, many VSPs do not to disclose information that truly reveals the ways in which they work in practice. Thus, achieving accountability through transparency becomes unlikely in these cases. At the same time, however, we also observe signiﬁcant interprogram variation (which we will examine in more detail). In preparation for the empirical analysis, we use a simple strategy to aggregate the available infor- mation and distill a synthetic, comprehensive transparency index that takes a unique value for each VSP. There- fore, we compute weighted averages of the disclosure dummies described above, across both the different areas of operations and the two levels of transparency. In the absence of a reﬁned theory on the issue, we weigh areas of operations symmetrically, assuming that they are all equally important in determining the overall proxy of trans- parency. In contrast, we allow for different weighting schemes of the two levels of transparency, following the common sense intuition that deep transparency is more important than shallow transparency. In order to provide a formal deﬁnition of the index, we ﬁrst introduce some simple notation. The area of operation (varying between decisionmaking, standard setting, veriﬁcation, and dispute settlement) is indexed with c while the level of transparency (either shallow or deep) is denoted with l. For each program s, area of operation c, and level l, we deﬁne a dummy variable ts; c, l that takes a value of 1 if the program s publicly discloses informa- tion at level l about area of operation c (and 0 otherwise). The transparency indicator is then constructed in three steps. First, we take a weighted sum of ts; c, l across areas and levels. Second, we divide the result by the number of areas of operation in which a program is active.
